   - How this codebase writes job/service tests (invocation pattern, factory dependencies)
   - Which factories exist and their key attributes
   - Any factories that do NOT yet exist and must be created as part of the feature
   Store the catalog in the epic's --description or as a comment on the epic issue. Isolated implementing agents read this at task-start to avoid redundant discovery of codebase patterns. This is orientation material, not a contract — implementing agents may deviate based on what they find.

3. Create TASK ISSUES as children of the molecule root epic:
   - Each task references its spec: --spec-id <spec-issue-id>
   - Outcome-based acceptance criteria (done when X, not when Y)
   - Failure modes (how this task fails)
   - Scope boundaries (what's NOT in this task)

4. Apply READY FOR DEVELOPMENT STANDARD to every task:
   - Actionable: file path + specific action
   - Logical: tasks ordered by dependency (use bd dep)
   - Testable: Given/When/Then covering happy path + edge cases
   - Complete: no placeholders, no TBDs
   - Self-Contained: a fresh Claude session can implement without conversation history

Label walking skeleton tasks with 'skeleton' and set priority P1.
Label remaining tasks with P2-P3.

Present the task list to the user for approval BEFORE creating beads issues.
When approved and tasks are created, close this step.

title: mol-feature
description: Full feature workflow: brainstorm → design → review → breakdown → skeleton → validation → build → ceremony retro → outcome check. Gates pause for human review at natural decision points. Learning loops close the feedback cycle.
notes: PARKED 2026-06-01 pending beads v1.0.6 (user decision). Review-discovery REJECTED the committed-JSONL design with 3 grounded blockers: B1 default bd export EXCLUDES memories (0/5; security-sensitive) so clone rehydrates 0 memories + the proposed skeleton would falsely pass; B2 concurrent same-bead edits write git conflict markers into issues.jsonl and bd import HARD-FAILS ('invalid character <') — no merge driver exists; B3 interactions.jsonl is tracked but bd export cannot emit it (no round-trip path). Strategic reframe (reviewer N2 + my analysis): Dolt-remote (Path B) dissolves B1/B2/B3 via cell-level merges + full-DB fidelity, and refs/dolt/* on origin satisfies 'lives with the repo' after the setup step we're building anyway. BUT Path B depends on multi-machine bd dolt sync, which v1.0.5 can unrecoverably corrupt and is only fixed in pending v1.0.6. RESUME TRIGGER: v1.0.6 released + dolt-sync fix verified. On resume: spike Dolt-remote round-trip (push refs/dolt to origin, fresh clone + fetch-refspec + bd dolt pull, verify full truth incl memories/interactions + a concurrent-edit merge); if green commit Path B, else fall back to Path A (committed-JSONL + custom union merge driver + memories security decision). Safe-now work that does NOT depend on sync model (clone-bootstrap script + announce-confirm auto-rehydrate; worktree discipline) was deferred with this park but could be split out if relief is needed sooner. Design docs: openspec/changes/beads-clone-shared-truth/.

title: Brainstorm: beads-clone-shared-truth
description:
Phase: THINK

Challenge whether this work should happen at all.

Problem: A fresh clone of this beads repo cannot see or work the shared bead truth: the committed issues.jsonl is present but bd reports 'no database found' until a manual bd init+import+hooks-install bootstrap, and ongoing use suffers export churn that blocks git operations, branch-switch state reverts, and noisy auto-resync commits — so the team cannot reliably work from a shared, in-repo source of truth.

Dispatch a team of named expert agents (TeamCreate + name + team_name) to challenge the idea from different angles. Use techniques from ~/.claude/skills/discovery/references/brainstorming-methods.csv and ~/.claude/skills/discovery/references/elicitation-methods.csv. Pick 1-2 techniques appropriate to the problem. Good defaults: First Principles Analysis, Pre-mortem Analysis, Assumption Reversal, Question Storming.

Agents should communicate via SendMessage and argue with each other. Relay their questions and findings to the user.

Possible outcomes (all tracked in beads):
1. BUILD — proceed to discovery step
2. DON'T BUILD — close molecule with reason. Record why.
3. DEFER — bd defer with context. Surface later when conditions change.
4. RECLASSIFY — this is actually a bug/chore. Close molecule, pour mol-rapid instead.
5. SPLIT — too big. Break into child epics first, then pour separate molecules.
6. DELEGATE — not our work. Record who owns it.
7. NEED MORE INFO — create a spike (type=spike) to investigate first.

For outcomes 2-7: close this molecule with --reason explaining the decision.
For outcome 1: close this step and proceed to discovery.

Do NOT show bd commands to the user. Present the challenge conversationally.
Do NOT shutdown agents until the user explicitly says they're done.
close_reason: Brainstorm complete. Framing: irreducible conflict = bd needs a binary Dolt DB (git-ignored, undiffable) but shared-truth-in-clone needs committed text → reconcile via clone-time rehydration from the already-committed issues.jsonl (NOT a Dolt-remote migration; refs/dolt/* aren't fetched by plain git clone). Decisions: (1) bootstrap UX = repo setup script + SessionStart auto-rehydrate; (2) scope = this repo first, then standardize to other JSONL-tracking repos. Riskiest assumption = issues.jsonl is lossless (clone+init+import reproduces identical issues/statuses/deps/memories); walking skeleton must diff rehydrated state vs source.

title: Gate: human discovery-approved
description: Async gate for step review-discovery

title: Review discovery: beads-clone-shared-truth
description:
Phase: DESIGN (decision point)

Two dispatches before presenting to the user:

1. ADVERSARIAL REVIEW — dispatch adversarial-reviewer subagent (isolated, no conversation history) to review the design. Sees ONLY the design content.

2. QA REVIEW (feature/epic only, skip for rapid) — dispatch a QA roundtable that starts from the Success Criteria section and works backward: 'What could prevent each success criterion from being achieved?'
   - feature: 3 named expert agents (TeamCreate + name + team_name), different domain lenses
   - epic: 5-8 named expert agents with domain-specific lenses (security, compliance, operations, developer experience, financial correctness, etc.)
   Agents argue via SendMessage. Their findings can change the scope — add non-goals, revise the walking skeleton, surface gaps in the success criteria themselves.
   Do NOT shutdown QA agents until the user explicitly says they're done.

Then present to the user:
- The adversarial reviewer's findings (BLOCK/CONCERN/NOTE)
- The QA roundtable's findings — especially any success criteria that the design doesn't address
- A plain-language summary of the design:
  - The success criteria and whether they're observable/falsifiable
  - The riskiest assumption and whether it's falsifiable
  - The walking skeleton scope (1-3 tasks, 30-60 min each)
  - The non-goals (are they specific enough?)

If either review found BLOCKs or unaddressed success criteria, recommend revision before approval.

User approves by saying 'looks good' or 'approved' — resolve the gate.
User can say 'revise' to send back to discovery.

Do NOT show gate IDs or bd commands to the user.

title: Discovery: beads-clone-shared-truth
description:
Phase: DESIGN

Run /discovery to produce an adversarial design for beads-clone-shared-truth.

Problem: A fresh clone of this beads repo cannot see or work the shared bead truth: the committed issues.jsonl is present but bd reports 'no database found' until a manual bd init+import+hooks-install bootstrap, and ongoing use suffers export churn that blocks git operations, branch-switch state reverts, and noisy auto-resync commits — so the team cannot reliably work from a shared, in-repo source of truth.

Before drafting, dispatch parallel named research agents (TeamCreate + name + team_name) to investigate:
- Existing code patterns relevant to this feature
- Similar past designs in docs/plans/ or openspec/changes/
- External API docs or constraints that affect the design

Agents report findings via SendMessage. Use their findings to inform the design.

The design must include:
1. Problem Statement
2. Success Criteria — numbered, observable, falsifiable outcomes. Not 'what we build' but 'what changes in the world when this ships.' Each criterion answers: 'A stakeholder would look at this and say yes, the problem is solved, because [specific observable state].' Minimum 3 for feature, minimum 5 for epic. These are the PRIMARY OUTPUT of discovery — everything else derives from them.
3. Non-Goals (min 3 — name what they lock in, not just what's excluded)
4. Riskiest Assumption (falsifiable bet with liveness test)
5. Walking Skeleton (1-3 tasks, 30-60 min each — each task traces to a success criterion)
6. Proof of Delivery (the single most important success criterion, stated as a sentence)
7. Anti-Metrics (what gets worse if we succeed wrong)
8. Future Increments

Output: openspec/changes/beads-clone-shared-truth/ (design.md, specs/, tasks.md).
The /discovery skill handles this automatically via the OpenSpec CLI.

When complete, close this step. The review gate will activate.
close_reason: Discovery complete. Wrote openspec/changes/beads-clone-shared-truth/{problem-framing,design}.md. Approach: rehydrate DB from committed issues.jsonl via (1) scripts/beads-setup.sh + (2) SessionStart auto-bootstrap; ongoing-sync hygiene + worktree discipline for friction. Riskiest assumption tested in a fresh clone: bd init+import rehydrated 38/38 issues, statuses preserved — issue+status losslessness confirmed; dep-edge+memory fidelity is the open item the walking skeleton (mol-w0q) closes. Non-goals: no Dolt-remote migration, no gitignoring jsonl, no committing the binary DB.

title: Review skeleton results: beads-clone-shared-truth
description:
Phase: VALIDATE (decision point)

Dispatch an adversarial-reviewer subagent (isolated, no conversation history) to review the skeleton code. The reviewer sees ONLY the changed files and test results.

Also dispatch a QA agent (TeamCreate + name + team_name) alongside the adversarial reviewer to produce system-level spec issues:
- Reads the design doc '## Risks and Edge Cases' section
- Reads the skeleton's changed files to understand the end-to-end flow that now exists
- Creates spec issues (--type spec, --parent <epic-id>) for integration scenarios that only become visible now that the skeleton exists: cross-component data flow, error propagation paths, concurrent access scenarios
- Scenarios must be testable against the skeleton code that exists RIGHT NOW — do not spec integration paths that require execute-full work to exist. If a cross-component path depends on a component not yet built, tag the spec issue :spec_only and note which execute-full task must complete before it becomes testable.
- Applies the same WHEN/THEN observable-outcome standard and scope circuit breaker as work-breakdown
- Does NOT re-cover what task-level spec issues already cover
These system-level specs become additional tasks if they reveal unaddressed risk.

This gate is CONDITIONAL:

- If all skeleton tasks closed as DONE with no concerns AND the adversarial reviewer found no BLOCKs:
  Auto-resolve this gate and proceed to full execution.
  Tell the user: 'Skeleton validated cleanly. Proceeding to full build.'

- If any skeleton task was DONE_WITH_CONCERNS or BLOCKED, or the reviewer found BLOCKs:
  Present the concerns + reviewer findings and ask the user to decide:
  'The skeleton found issues: [summary]. Proceed, revise, or stop?'
  User approves → resolve gate.
  User says revise → create amendment tasks, update design.
  User says stop → close molecule with reason.

Do NOT show gate IDs or bd commands to the user.

title: Execute remaining tasks: beads-clone-shared-truth
description:
Phase: BUILD

BEFORE dispatching implementing agents, run a SYSTEM-LEVEL QA PASS (this is Tier 3 — cross-component integration specs):
- Dispatch a QA agent (TeamCreate + name + team_name) that reads: the validated skeleton code, the design doc's cross-component flow descriptions, and all existing spec issues
- Generates system-level spec issues for integration scenarios that span multiple components: the full activation pipeline, cross-org isolation, error propagation across service boundaries, concurrent access paths
- This is the right moment — the skeleton has proven the architecture works, all components will exist by end of execute-full, and no implementing agents have started yet. Post-skeleton was too early (minimal code); end-of-execute-full is too late (chicken-and-egg with spec audit)
- Applies the same WHEN/THEN observable-outcome standard and scope circuit breaker as work-breakdown
- These system-level spec issues become tasks in the first or second wave

Acceptance criteria define OUTCOMES, not test implementations. An implementing agent satisfies a criterion if their test verifies the same observable outcome — the assertion code may differ from what the criterion literally describes. 'campaign.start_date equals template.start_date' can be verified as `expect(campaign.reload.start_date).to eq(template.start_date)` or any equivalent assertion. Criteria constrain the outcome; the developer chooses the verification approach.

Run /beads-execution to dispatch parallel named agents (TeamCreate + name + team_name) for ALL remaining tasks. Dispatch in dependency-aware waves — all unblocked tasks in a wave get their own named agent working simultaneously.

Query to find remaining tasks:
  bd list --parent <molecule-root-id> --status open
  (skeleton tasks are already closed)

If agents discover assumption failures during execution:
- Surface via BLOCKED or DONE_WITH_CONCERNS
- Create amendment tasks if the design needs revision
- When an amendment updates a spec issue's --acceptance field: (1) the amendment task description MUST note 'Spec issue [id] --acceptance criteria have changed — re-read before the next RED test'; (2) all task issues referencing this spec via --spec-id are affected — list them explicitly so the developer knows which tasks are touched. Task amendments that do NOT change --acceptance (e.g., implementation approach, file location, effort) leave existing test criteria valid.
- Do not silently work around problems

When all tasks complete:
1. SPEC AUDIT — Dispatch a named agent to systematically walk through EVERY spec scenario (bd list --parent <molecule-root-id> --type spec). For each WHEN/THEN contract, verify the implementation satisfies it. This is not a code review — it is a spec-to-implementation gap check. Any unmet scenario becomes a new task.
2. Dispatch adversarial-reviewer subagent (isolated, no conversation history) on the complete implementation — all changed files, all test results.
3. Run proof-of-delivery verification:
   - Execute the actual workflow the user cares about
   - Check against anti-metrics from the design
   - Verify the proof of delivery sentence is satisfied
4. Present spec audit gaps + reviewer findings + verification results to the user.

Only close this step when ALL spec scenarios pass, the adversarial reviewer has reported, AND proof of delivery is verified.

title: ScheduleWakeup does not satisfy continuation-harness Stop gate; stale fallbacks replay completed work
description: Observed 2026-05-29. Two related defects in the ScheduleWakeup <-> continuation-harness interaction: (1) The harness Stop gate (continuation-harness.md path 2) expects a wakeup entry in ~/.claude/harness/threads/{session_id}/scheduled.json, but calls to the ScheduleWakeup tool did NOT satisfy the gate — every wait-turn while legitimately blocked on background work still got 'no_completion_or_resumption_proof' and a Stop block. So path 2 (the documented resumption escape) is effectively non-functional via the ScheduleWakeup tool; only verify-pass (path 1) or user-release (path 3) actually release the gate. (2) Fallback wakeups fire on their timer regardless of whether the primary work already finished, re-injecting the same /loop prompt — so a late fallback replays an already-completed task list (observed: a 05:30 fallback fired at 09:05 asking to redo the entire fxh remediation, all already done). Fix: make ScheduleWakeup write the scheduled.json entry the harness Stop gate reads (or teach would_block_stop.py to recognize ScheduleWakeup's own store), AND have fallback wakeups no-op / self-cancel when their tracked work already completed.
acceptance_criteria: A ScheduleWakeup call satisfies the harness Stop gate's resumption path (no spurious no_completion blocks while a registered wakeup exists), AND a fallback wakeup whose underlying work already completed does not replay a finished task list.
close_reason: Done + merged in PR #6; re-closing after the main-sync reverted the status again (beads working-tree coupling, tracked as mol-dtt).

title: Background Workflow runs die silently at ~13-min host task timeout (no completion signal)
description: Observed 2026-05-29 during the fxh-remediation workflow (run wf_847dcdad-5cc): the background Workflow process terminated TWICE at ~13 minutes elapsed — once mid-fxh.7 (after editing 8 of 12 hooks, before closing the bead), once mid-fxh.12 (after writing the deliverable, before closing). No completion notification fired; the orchestrator only discovered the death via a fallback ScheduleWakeup + manual ps/file-activity inspection. Resume-from-runId worked (cached completed agents) but a third death would have recurred at the same timeout. Impact: long multi-agent workflows (heavy cross-cutting phases like a 12-hook rewrite) cannot complete in one background run and require manual death-detection + resume or manual finish. Investigate the host task-timeout for local_workflow background tasks, and either (a) raise/remove it for workflows, (b) emit a clear timeout/death notification so the parent is re-invoked instead of silently stranded, and/or (c) have the workflow runner checkpoint+auto-resume across the timeout boundary.
acceptance_criteria: A workflow whose total wall-clock exceeds the current ~13-min limit either completes, or the parent agent receives an explicit death/timeout notification (not silence) and can resume without manual ps/file-activity forensics.
close_reason: Done + merged in PR #6; re-closing after the main-sync reverted the status again (beads working-tree coupling, tracked as mol-dtt).

title: [gate-monitor] mock-bureaucracy risk: _test_fake_gate
description:
Gate `_test_fake_gate` shows mock-bureaucracy risk in the last 1d: 3 waivers vs 1 denies (ratio 3.0).

Per `claude/rules/gate-design.md` Rule 3 (validate the value, not just the presence) and the Wiesche et al. (2013) finding that both coercive AND enabling designs can produce mock bureaucracy: this ratio suggests symbolic compliance is taking over from real enforcement.

## What to check

1. Read the captured waiver reasons:
   `python3 ~/.claude/bin/gate_signal_query.py --gate _test_fake_gate --decision waiver-accepted --since 30d`
2. If the reasons cluster on a specific pattern, the gate's heuristic is too aggressive — prune or refine.
3. If the reasons are diverse / ad-hoc, the gate may be firing correctly and the user is overriding for context reasons; consider whether the gate's denial message could better explain *why* the rule applies in this case.

title: test_oracle_brief_gate.py — revise per gate audit (hard deny with no contest path)
description:
Friction-analyst panel: gate PASSES Repair, Internal Transparency, Global Transparency, but FAILS Flexibility — no documented waiver or escape. Coercive drift risk.

When this gate fires on a quick bug fix in a repo where TDD is exempt, the agent has no path forward except producing a low-quality brief just to clear the gate. Textbook mock bureaucracy: the brief is created for compliance, not quality.

## Recommended fix

Per gate-design.md Rule 1 (every gate must have a first-class escape path): add an 'ask' escape analogous to tdd-gate.py's 'say proceed to skip TDD for this change.'

Specifically:
- Add 'say proceed to skip the oracle brief for this change' as a documented inline override
- Reason text required (not a checkbox)
- Reason persists to .beads/.gate-waivers.jsonl per Rule 2

## Acceptance

- A documented agent-invokable escape exists in the denial message itself
- The escape requires (and persists) a substantive reason
- A brief produced in 30 seconds to clear a block is no longer the only path forward
close_reason: test_oracle_brief_gate revised: hard deny converted to 'ask' with documented 'say proceed to skip' escape. Standard convention now matches tdd-gate, outcome_assertion_gate, discovery-gate. Signal capture at both decision branches. Friction-analyst's recommendation implemented.

title: implementation_echo_test_gate.py — revise per gate audit (high-confidence heuristic, no override)
description:
Friction-analyst panel: same pattern as test_oracle_brief_gate.py. Hard deny on detected implementation-echo patterns, no contest path.

The 'high-confidence' heuristic in the source code suggests the author knew false positives were possible, but the agent receiving a denial has no 'I disagree with this classification' channel. A legitimate use of a shared constant that lexically resembles a generated ID gets the same hard block.

## Recommended fix

Per gate-design.md Rule 1: add an override path requiring a written justification.

Friction-analyst's specific proposal: 'If this detection is incorrect, add a comment `# oracle: <one sentence explaining why this constant is an independent oracle>` to the assertion line and rerun.'

This turns contested detections into labeled training data for improving the heuristic (per Rule 2 — signal persistence), and the comment-required mechanism prevents the escape from being used carelessly.

## Acceptance

- A documented agent-invokable override exists for contested detections
- The override requires a comment with a substantive reason
- The reason text is captured (either by the gate itself or by code review) so it becomes labeled training data
close_reason: implementation_echo_test_gate revised: per-file # oracle: <reason> override implemented (Python and JS/TS comment forms). Hard deny converted to 'ask' with both rewrite-the-test and override-via-comment paths documented in the message. Override reasons captured as labeled training data. Signal capture at all three decision branches. Friction-analyst's recommendation implemented in full.

title: oracle_downgrade_warning_gate.py — revise per gate audit (unverifiable honor system)
description:
Integrator panel: gate is advisory ask, structurally flexible, BUT the escape condition ('proceed only if Test Oracle Brief has been updated or replacement is equal-or-stronger oracle') is unverifiable by the gate. Agent under pressure will say 'brief was updated' and proceed — symbolic compliance without enforcement.

This is mock-bureaucracy-risk (Wiesche et al. 2013) — the gate creates the *illusion* of a documented justification because the condition is stated, but no mechanism verifies the claim.

## Recommended fix

Per gate-design.md Rule 3 (validate the value, not the presence): the escape condition must be either verifiable mechanically or replaced with a captured-reason waiver.

Concretely: either (a) check that the test-oracle brief file at .agent/runtime/test-oracle-brief.md was modified in the last 24 hours, OR (b) require the agent to provide a 'state your reason' string that persists to .beads/.gate-waivers.jsonl per gate-design Rule 2.

## Acceptance

- Escape condition is either mechanically verifiable (file mtime check or similar) OR captures a reason as labeled signal
- Mock-bureaucracy path 'proceed if brief updated' no longer exists in the denial without a backing check
close_reason: oracle_downgrade_warning_gate revised: unverifiable honor-system text replaced with mechanically-verifiable check (.agent/runtime/test-oracle-brief.md modified in last 24h). Two explicit paths forward in the denial: update the brief OR say 'proceed' (captured). Signal capture at both decision branches. Integrator's mock-bureaucracy-risk finding addressed.

title: Investigate openspec artifact commit discipline — discovery vs implementation agent gap
description:
## Problem

openspec artifacts (design docs, specs, tasks.md) generated during discovery sessions regularly go untracked and uncommitted to git. Two examples surfaced 2026-06-03 in the cake repo: dws-device-confidence-layer and salesforce-client-seam-extraction both had full design docs + specs that were never staged when the work shipped.

The root cause is structural: the agent that writes the discovery artifacts (in a design session) is often different from the agent that later implements the work. The implementing agent never sees the untracked openspec directory and has no prompt to commit it.

## Questions to investigate

- Can a pre-commit hook or openspec_init_guard detect untracked openspec/changes/ directories and warn before commits that touch related code?
- Should mol-feature / openspec-apply-change include an explicit step to commit openspec artifacts as part of the implementation PR?
- When a second agent picks up a bead mid-molecule, what context tells it that related openspec artifacts exist locally but are untracked?
- Is there a reliable pattern for co-locating discovery artifacts with their implementing PR across multi-session / multi-agent workflows?

## Context

- The convention establishes that openspec artifacts belong in git alongside the code they describe
- The Jira-ticket gate on PRs catches missing ticket refs but nothing currently catches missing/untracked openspec artifacts
- This is part of the broader multi-agent handoff problem: filesystem state is invisible to a new agent picking up a bead mid-molecule unless it is in git or beads

title: Ceremony retrospective: oracle-independence
description:
Phase: LEARN

Dispatch parallel named agents (TeamCreate + name + team_name) to evaluate different dimensions simultaneously:

1. ASSUMPTION ACCURACY: Was the riskiest assumption actually the hardest part?
   bd audit record --kind assumption_retrospective --issue-id <epic-id>

2. SPEC ACCURACY: Did specs predict the real edge cases? Two signals:
   a. Spec misses: count WHEN/THEN scenarios that were absent from --acceptance but caught during implementation (task close notes are the source)
   b. Acceptance-criteria fidelity: how often did the implementing agent's test match the work-breakdown acceptance criteria? Divergence = the criteria were underspecified or the code context changed the right assertion shape. High divergence = work-breakdown is producing stubs too early; low divergence = criteria are well-calibrated.
   bd audit record --kind spec_accuracy --issue-id <epic-id>

3. TASK GRANULARITY: Were tasks the right size?
   - Any split mid-implementation? (too big)
   - Any closed in <10 min? (too small)
   bd audit record --kind task_granularity --issue-id <epic-id>

4. CEREMONY WEIGHT: One question to the user:
   'Was there a moment where the process slowed you down instead of helping?'
   If yes → bd remember the feedback.
   If no → that's also signal. Record it.

5. FORMULA EVOLUTION: If the actual workflow diverged meaningfully from mol-feature:
   bd mol distill to extract the improved pattern.

This step auto-closes after recording findings. No gate.

title: validate_no_shirking: 'without' certainty-idiom wrongly suppresses real shirking
description: QC (qc-shirking; fxh.2) found 'without' is in _NEGATION_CUES, but 'Without a doubt ...' / 'Without question ...' are CERTAINTY idioms, the inverse of disavowal. So 'Without a doubt this is a pre-existing failure unrelated to us.' is wrongly SUPPRESSED (window 'Without a doubt this is a', cue 'without', no clause break -> guarded). Narrower than the clause-scope hole already fixed (requires opening with a certainty idiom). Fix: drop 'without' from negation cues, or require it to be followed by a clause break within N words. Pre-existing pattern-coverage gaps also noted ('pre-existing and unrelated' without 'failure' doesn't match; 'leaving for now' without 'this' doesn't match) — separate from the guard.
close_reason: Closed

title: Harden _is_safe_session_id against NUL bytes in enforce_named_agents
description: QC (qc-named-final, fxh.2) found _is_safe_session_id('a\x00b') returns True — a NUL byte is not screened, unlike path separators. Not exploitable today (an embedded NUL makes open() raise ValueError, which the tracker funcs catch/swallow → degrades to no-tracking, never escapes the dir), but the docstring claims to reject 'anything that could escape the tracker directory' and NUL is a classic filename-injection byte. Add '\x00' in value to the reject set, with a test.
close_reason: Closed

title: outcome_assertion_gate.py — revise per gate audit (signal capture missing)
description:
Integrator panel: gate is well-designed at the interaction level (PASS Repair, Internal Transparency, Flexibility via 'say proceed') but missing Global Transparency reference and Rule 2 signal capture.

Risk: agents learn to say 'proceed' reflexively without engaging the substance — the waiver becomes a checkbox.

## Recommended fix

Per gate-design.md Rule 2 (every gate produces persistent signal):

1. Add a pointer from claude/rules/ (or directly in the denial) to tdd-enforcement.md explaining the structural-vs-outcome assertion distinction.
2. Capture 'proceed' waiver reasons to .beads/.gate-waivers.jsonl with the test function names that triggered. Even if the reason is one word, the captured event is signal.

## Acceptance

- Gate's role is discoverable from a rule file or denial message
- Waiver invocations persist with the matched-test-functions context

title: context_burn_detector.py — revise per gate audit (signal evaporates, not discoverable)
description:
Integrator panel: advisory-only hook, never blocks, but two issues:

1. No corresponding rule in claude/rules/ — the 'resets on Agent dispatch' mechanic is non-obvious and not documented anywhere user-visible.
2. Nudge fires once per session at threshold crossing — agent that ignores it faces no further friction, and the signal evaporates into conversation context.

Mock-bureaucracy-risk: this is the operating rule failure in its purest form (gate produces signal that goes nowhere).

## Recommended fix

Per gate-design.md Rule 2 (signal persistence) + the bureaucracy principle's Global Transparency design feature:

1. Create claude/rules/agent-dispatch-preference.md (or extend serena-first.md) to document why this gate exists and the 'resets on Agent dispatch' behavior.
2. Persist the cost-accumulator to .beads/.context-burn.log so multi-session patterns are visible.
3. Consider whether 'once per threshold crossing' is the right tradeoff — a visible counter ('cost = 23 / 30') in subsequent operations might produce more behavioral change than a single-fire nudge.

## Acceptance

- Gate has a corresponding entry in claude/rules/ that explains its purpose
- Cost-accumulator events persist beyond the session
- The reset-on-Agent-dispatch behavior is documented

title: review_gate.py — revise per gate audit (missing internal transparency)
description:
Friction-analyst panel: gate is advisory only (good Repair, good Global Transparency, good Flexibility) but Internal Transparency FAILS — the warning says 'consider dispatching' without explaining *why* review matters here.

Result: an agent receiving this for the first time has no idea what kind of review satisfies it (blinded? any reviewer word? subagent_type?). Risks mock bureaucracy: agent dispatches a trivially-named reviewer just to clear the warning.

## Recommended fix

Per gate-design.md Rule 1 (the escape is documented in the denial itself) + the bureaucracy principle's Internal Transparency design feature:

Add one sentence to _WARN_NO_REVIEW naming what review is protecting against (catching drift between spec and implementation, catching oracle downgrades) and the two signals that satisfy the gate (subagent_type containing 'reviewer' OR review-word in name/description).

## Acceptance

- Warning message includes the rationale (what review protects against)
- Warning names the two ways an agent satisfies the gate
close_reason: Closed by review_gate revise. The _WARN_NO_REVIEW message now includes the rationale (what review catches) and the two satisfaction signals (subagent_type or review-word). Signal capture added at both decision branches via the gate-signal-persistence-foundation. Friction-analyst's specific recommendation implemented in full.

title: discovery-close-gate.py — revise per gate audit (mtime petrification + session-level fatigue)
description:
Adversary panel: gate is structurally well-designed (ask, not deny; reflects user's own past commitments) but two bloat-risk issues:

1. 90-day mtime cutoff is the same oracle-downgrade pattern the kaizen just removed from discovery-gate.py (commit 3f8d37b). A 91-day-old design that's still authoritative becomes invisible.
2. Same prompts fire repeatedly on consecutive closes against the same design — agent habituates → mock bureaucracy via fatigue.

## Recommended fix

Per delicate-art-of-bureaucracy.md Operating Rule 1 (every rule has a half-life) + gate-design.md (escape paths first-class):

1. Drop the 90-day mtime filter; use existence + git-tracked status instead.
2. Deduplicate within a session: track which design's prompts have fired this session and skip repeats.
3. Add 'from your design at <path>' to the prompt header for global transparency.

## Acceptance

- No mtime cutoff in the hook
- Repeated closes against the same design within a session don't re-ask the same prompts
- Prompts reference the source design-doc path explicitly
close_reason: discovery-close-gate revised: 90-day mtime cutoff removed (petrification), within-session dedup added (anti-fatigue), global-transparency header added to prompts, signal capture at all three branches. All three audit findings resolved in one commit.

title: enforce_named_agents.py — revise per gate audit (NEVER absolutism, no waiver path)
description:
Adversary panel: gate has BEST Repair UX of the audited set (denial shows exact correct invocation pattern inline) but Flexibility FAILS — the rule says 'There is NEVER a reason to dispatch an anonymous agent' which is the most absolutist statement in any of the audited denials.

Two legitimate cases the hook can't accommodate: (1) one-off fire-and-forget research agent for a one-line lookup where naming overhead exceeds value, (2) user explicitly requests a quick anonymous probe. Petrified-leaning.

## Recommended fix

Per gate-design.md Rule 1 (escape paths first-class) + Rule 2 (signal persistence):

1. Soften 'NEVER' to 'almost never, and here's the escape': name='oneoff' or name='probe' is acceptable.
2. Expose the 30-second window countdown in the soft nudge ('9 more seconds until block').
3. Add a documented waiver flag for legitimate anonymous dispatch with reason capture.

## Acceptance

- Denial rationale no longer uses absolutist 'NEVER' language
- A documented one-off escape path exists (e.g., name='oneoff' accepted with a reason captured)
- The hidden 30-second tracker state is exposed in the nudge before the block
close_reason: enforce_named_agents revised: 'NEVER' absolutism softened to 'almost never, and here's the escape' with two specific legitimate cases named (one-off, user-requested probe), the throwaway-name pattern (name='oneoff' or 'probe') documented. Signal capture added at all four decision branches. The dynamic countdown follow-on (replace static '30 seconds' with live timer) is noted in commit but deferred — the main UX concern is addressed by the softened text.
